chunks.text:
# What Is Conversational AI? ZeroShot Bot CEO Jason Mars Explains

Entrepreneur Jason Mars calls conversation our “first technology.”

meta.section: What Is Conversational AI? ZeroShot Bot CEO Jason Mars Explains

chunks.text: Before humans invented the wheel, crafted a spear or tamed fire, we mastered the superpower of talking to one another.

meta.section: What Is Conversational AI? ZeroShot Bot CEO Jason Mars Explains

chunks.text: That makes conversation an incredibly important tool.

meta.section: What Is Conversational AI? ZeroShot Bot CEO Jason Mars Explains

chunks.text: But if you've dealt with the automated chatbots deployed by the customer service arms of just about any big organization lately — whether banks or airlines — you also know how hard it can be to get it right.

meta.section: What Is Conversational AI? ZeroShot Bot CEO Jason Mars Explains
What Is Conversational AI? ZeroShot Bot CEO Jason Mars Explains

chunks.text: [Deep learning AI](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/deep-learning-2/) and new techniques such as zero-shot learning promise to change that.

meta.section: What Is Conversational AI? ZeroShot Bot CEO Jason Mars Explains

chunks.text: On this episode of NVIDIA’s [AI Podcast](https://blogs.nvidia.com/ai-podcast/), host Noah Kravitz — whose intelligence is anything but artificial — spoke with Mars about how the latest AI techniques intersect with the very ancient art of conversation.

meta.section: What Is Conversational AI? ZeroShot Bot CEO Jason Mars Explains

chunks.text: In addition to being an entrepreneur and CEO of several startups, including Zero Shot Bot, Mars is an associate professor of computer science at the University of Michigan and the author of “Breaking Bots: Inventing a New Voice in the AI Revolution” (ForbesBooks, 2021).

meta.section: What Is Conversational AI? ZeroShot Bot CEO Jason Mars Explains

chunks.text: This article was originally posted on nvidia.com, click the link below to read the complete article.

meta.section: What Is Conversational AI? ZeroShot Bot CEO Jason Mars Explains

chunks.text: [click here](https://blogs.nvidia.com/blog/2022/04/27/what-is-conversational-ai-jason-mars/)

meta.section: What Is Conversational AI? ZeroShot Bot CEO Jason Mars Explains

chunks.text:
# What 75 students shipped in one semester with Jac

The University of Michigan's CSE division just published a [story on the final showcase for **EECS 449: Conversational AI**](https://cse.engin.umich.edu/stories/students-build-agentic-ai-tools-for-work-travel-wellness), where **75+ students**, working in teams, built **17 full-stack AI applications** in a single Winter semester, most of them in [Jac](https://www.jaseci.org/).

meta.section: What 75 students shipped in one semester with Jac
What 75 students shipped in one semester with Jac

chunks.text: **These products aren't chatbot demos.** They are live and full-stack: conversational interfaces, visual reasoning, document analysis, multimodal input, and multi-step agentic workflows, built for everything from academic advising and health to travel, finance, and career prep.

meta.section: What 75 students shipped in one semester with Jac

chunks.text:
## One language, one semester

Building a full AI application usually means stitching together separate systems: one framework for the interface, another for the backend, a database layer underneath, and more tooling to call models and orchestrate workflows. Each seam is its own thing to learn, wire up, and keep from breaking, and for a four-person team on a deadline, a lot of the semester can disappear into that glue work instead of the product.

meta.section: One language, one semester

chunks.text: That is exactly where Jac came in. Its single-language design is a big reason a semester was enough: Jac and the Jaseci runtime let a team handle the interface, the application logic, the data, and the AI itself in one language. AI calls are part of the language rather than a bolted-on service, and Jac stays model-agnostic underneath, so teams could reach for whatever model fit without re-architecting anything. Less time on plumbing, more on the actual problem.

meta.section: One language, one semester

chunks.text:
## From the showcase

The range shows what Jaseci made possible. Here are three standouts:

meta.section: From the showcase

chunks.text: [**Skooch.ai**](http://skooch.ai) is an adaptive scheduling assistant that learns someone's focus windows and energy patterns, then places flexible tasks around fixed commitments from a plain-language prompt like "build me a study plan."

meta.section: From the showcase

chunks.text: [**MaizeMind.com**](http://maizemind.com) turns half-formed notes into an interactive argument map, surfacing claims, evidence, and contradictions without ghostwriting, with Jac's graph data model mapping directly onto the argument structure.

meta.section: From the showcase

chunks.text: [**EdgeCastApp.com**](http://edgecastapp.com) is a research terminal for prediction-market traders that links live Kalshi markets to a real-time newsfeed and an agent that already knows which market you're viewing.

meta.section: From the showcase

chunks.text: The same story sits underneath all three: Jaseci was the common thread that made them feasible on a student timeline, letting each team keep the interface, the logic, the data, and the AI in one application instead of a stack of integrations.

meta.section: From the showcase

chunks.text: You can explore the full lineup, with every team, demo videos, live apps, and GitHub repos, on the [EECS 449 projects showcase](https://jaseci-labs.github.io/eecs-449-projects/), and read more in the [U-M story](https://cse.engin.umich.edu/stories/students-build-agentic-ai-tools-for-work-travel-wellness).

meta.section: From the showcase

chunks.text:
<div class="cta cta-rich" markdown="1">
**Want to build something full-stack in a single language?** Install [Jac](https://www.jaseci.org/) and scaffold your first app in a few minutes, with the backend, frontend, and AI in one codebase, the same setup behind these 17 student projects.

meta.section: From the showcase

chunks.text: [Try it yourself](https://docs.jaseci.org/){ .cta-button }

meta.section: From the showcase

chunks.text:
# Browser Automation Has a Data Model Problem. Walkers Fix It.

Every browser automation script you've written has the same hidden bug: it doesn't know what it's doing. It knows the steps. It doesn't know the structure.

meta.section: Browser Automation Has a Data Model Problem. Walkers Fix It.

chunks.text: You write `page.goto()`, then `page.fill()`, then `page.click()`. Each call succeeds or fails on its own. The script has no idea that it just navigated to a login page, that the text field it filled was a username input, or that the button it clicked submits a form. It's running commands against a stateless API. The moment the page structure changes, a new modal shows up, an element gets renamed, there's an extra redirect, the script breaks. Not because you got the logic wrong. There just wasn't any logic. Just steps.

meta.section: Browser Automation Has a Data Model Problem. Walkers Fix It.

chunks.text: This is where browser automation sits in 2026. Playwright, Selenium, Browser-use, Stagehand, they all give you good primitives. None of them give you a model for what a browser session actually *is*. State lives in local variables. Context evaporates between function calls. The relationship between a session, its pages, and their elements exists only in your head.

meta.section: Browser Automation Has a Data Model Problem. Walkers Fix It.

chunks.text: I built [jac-browser](https://github.com/jaseci-labs/jac-agentbrowser) to try to fix this. It's a browser automation library written entirely in Jac, 153 walkers that wrap Playwright. But the wrapper isn't really the point. The point is that browser sessions are graphs, and Jac gives you graphs as a first-class language construct. The result is a library where the data model *is* the automation framework.

meta.section: Browser Automation Has a Data Model Problem. Walkers Fix It.
Browser Automation Has a Data Model Problem. Walkers Fix It.
Browser Automation Has a Data Model Problem. Walkers Fix It.

chunks.text:
## Four tools, one problem

Let's be fair to the existing tools. They're good at what they do.

meta.section: Four tools, one problem

chunks.text: **Playwright** is fast, reliable, and has the best auto-wait system out there. **Selenium** has ecosystem reach that nothing else touches, every language, every CI system, every tutorial. **Browser-use** and **Stagehand** are doing interesting work: they let LLMs drive browsers using vision models and natural language, which opens up automation to people who can't write selectors.

meta.section: Four tools, one problem

chunks.text: But they all make the same architectural assumption: the browser is a stateless function call target. You call methods on page objects. The page objects are opaque handles. There's no structured representation of what's on the page, how pages relate to each other, or what happened three steps ago.

meta.section: Four tools, one problem

chunks.text: This is the root of the fragility that makes browser automation painful at scale.

meta.section: Four tools, one problem

chunks.text:
| Feature | Playwright | Selenium | Browser-use | Stagehand | jac-browser |
|---------|-----------|----------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| Actions | ~50 methods | ~40 methods | LLM-driven | LLM-driven | 153 walkers |
| Data model | None (handles) | None (handles) | None | None | Session graph |
| AI agent support | Manual | Manual | Vision model | Vision model | ARIA snapshot-ref |
| Multi-step patterns | DIY | DIY | Prompt-based | Prompt-based | 8 composite walkers |
| Cost per action | Free | Free | LLM API call | LLM API call | Free |
| REST API mode | No | Selenium Grid | No | No | `jac serve` (zero code changes) |

meta.section: Four tools, one problem

chunks.text: The "Data model" row is the one worth paying attention to. Everything else follows from it.

meta.section: Four tools, one problem
Four tools, one problem

chunks.text:
## You built a graph and called it a class

If you've done serious browser automation, you've probably used the Page Object Model. Standard pattern: create a class for each page, store element locators as properties, expose actions as methods.

meta.section: You built a graph and called it a class
You built a graph and called it a class

chunks.text: Look at what this code actually models. `LoginPage` holds references to elements. It returns a `DashboardPage`, a navigation relationship. `DashboardPage` holds its own elements. The `page` handle threads through everything, representing a session.

meta.section: You built a graph and called it a class

chunks.text: This is a graph. Sessions contain pages. Pages contain elements. Pages connect to other pages through navigation. You modeled a graph and encoded it in class hierarchies because your language didn't give you a better option.

meta.section: You built a graph and called it a class

chunks.text:
```mermaid
graph TD
    Session["Browser Session"]
    LP["LoginPage"]
    DP["DashboardPage"]
    U["#username"]
    P["#password"]
    S["submit button"]
    NL["nav links"]
    UM["user menu"]

    Session --> LP
    Session --> DP
    LP --> U
    LP --> P
    LP --> S
    LP -.->|"login() returns"| DP
    DP --> NL
    DP --> UM

    style Session fill:#333,stroke:#666,color:#fff
    style LP fill:#555,stroke:#888,color:#fff
    style DP fill:#555,stroke:#888,color:#fff
```

meta.section: You built a graph and called it a class

chunks.text: The Page Object Model is basically an ad-hoc, poorly-modeled graph. Every PageObject class is a node. Every element locator is a child node. Every method that returns another PageObject is an edge. The "pattern" is just OOP compensating for the lack of spatial data structures.

meta.section: You built a graph and called it a class

chunks.text: What if you just made the graph explicit?

meta.section: You built a graph and called it a class
You built a graph and called it a class

chunks.text:
## Sessions contain pages. Pages contain elements. That's a graph.

Jac is a programming language built around the idea that some problems are naturally graph-shaped, and your language should make graphs as easy to work with as lists or dictionaries. Nodes and edges are first-class types. Walkers, mobile units of computation, traverse the graph, executing logic at each node they visit.

meta.section: Sessions contain pages. Pages contain elements. That's a graph.

chunks.text: If you haven't seen Jac before, here's the short version: instead of calling methods on objects (OOP), you send walkers to visit nodes arranged in a graph (Object-Spatial Programming). The walker carries the logic. The nodes carry the data. The graph defines the structure.

meta.section: Sessions contain pages. Pages contain elements. That's a graph.

chunks.text: Browser automation maps onto this pretty naturally.

meta.section: Sessions contain pages. Pages contain elements. That's a graph.

chunks.text: <div class="code-block">

meta.section: Sessions contain pages. Pages contain elements. That's a graph.
Sessions contain pages. Pages contain elements. That's a graph.
